title: Binary Search
description: The O(log n) algorithm promised back in Big O Notation: cutting the search space in half with every step, on a sorted array.
objectives: Trace binary search step by step
Understand why it achieves O(log n)
Know binary search requires a sorted input

sections.text: 1. Recap: This Was Promised in Big O Notation
Big O Notation previewed O(log n) with: "Binary Search... cuts the remaining problem roughly in half with each step." Here it is.
2. The Problem: Linear Search Is O(n)
For a sorted array specifically, checking every element wastes information — the sorted order itself tells us a lot about where the target could possibly be.
Binary search finds a target in a sorted array by repeatedly checking the middle element and discarding the half of the remaining range that cannot contain the target.

sections.title: Tracing it (target = 23)
points: left=0, right=7, mid=3 → numbers[3]=12, too small → left = 4
left=4, right=7, mid=5 → numbers[5]=23, found! → return 5

sections.text: mid = left + (right - left) / 2 is deliberately written this way instead of (left + right) / 2 — for very large arrays, left + right could theoretically overflow int (recall Type Modifiers), while this form avoids that risk entirely.
5. Why O(log n): Halving Each Step

Every comparison eliminates half of whatever range remains. Starting with n elements: n → n/2 → n/4 → n/8 → ... → 1. The number of halvings needed to shrink n down to 1 is exactly log₂n — this is the direct source of binary search's O(log n) complexity, and exactly the shape previewed in Big O Notation.

Binary search's correctness depends entirely on the array being sorted — that's what guarantees "if numbers[mid] is too small, the target can only be to the right" is actually true. Running binary search on an unsorted array gives unreliable results, since that guarantee no longer holds.

sections.title: Looking up a word in a physical dictionary
sections.text: Nobody looks up a word in a dictionary by reading every page from the start — you open somewhere in the middle, see whether your word comes before or after that page alphabetically, and jump to the middle of the correct remaining half. Repeating that a few times finds any word almost instantly, in a dictionary of any size — precisely binary search's logarithmic behavior.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Running binary search on an unsorted array — the technique's correctness assumes sorted order.
Using while (left < right) instead of while (left <= right), missing the case where left equals right (a single remaining candidate).
Forgetting to update left = mid + 1 or right = mid - 1 — reusing mid itself in the next range causes an infinite loop.
Writing (left + right) / 2 instead of left + (right - left) / 2 — a theoretical overflow risk for very large ranges.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is binary search, and what does it require of its input?
questions.answer: An algorithm that finds a target in a sorted array by repeatedly checking the middle element and discarding the half that cannot contain the target — it requires the array to be sorted.

questions.question: What is binary search's time complexity, and why?
questions.answer: O(log n) — because each comparison halves the remaining search range, and it takes log₂n halvings to shrink n elements down to one.

questions.question: Why is mid = left + (right - left) / 2 preferred over (left + right) / 2?
questions.answer: It avoids a theoretical integer overflow when left and right are both large, since (left + right) could exceed int's range before the division happens.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Binary search finds a target in a sorted array by repeatedly checking the middle element and discarding half of the remaining range based on whether the target is smaller or larger — left = mid + 1 or right = mid - 1. Because each step halves the remaining search space, it takes only log₂n steps to narrow n elements down to one, giving O(log n) time, exactly the complexity class previewed in Big O Notation. Binary search's correctness depends entirely on the array being sorted; running it on unsorted data gives unreliable results.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Binary Search
sections.text: Binary search = looking up a word in a dictionary. Check the middle, discard the wrong half, repeat. Requires sorted order. O(log n) — halved every step.

sections.title: Binary Search
sections.problemStatement: Given a sorted array of integers nums and a target, return the index of target, or -1 if not found.
hints: This is the exact algorithm this lesson just taught — no variation needed.
left/right pointers bracket the search range; mid splits it in half each iteration.
target smaller than nums[mid] means search the left half; larger means search the right half.

approaches.label: Brute Force
Optimal
approaches.explanation: Standard binary search, halving the range each step.

sections.title: Search Insert Position
sections.problemStatement: Given a sorted array nums and a target, return the index if found, or the index where it would be inserted to keep the array sorted.
hints: This is regular binary search, but instead of returning -1 on failure, something useful about WHERE the search ended is needed.
When the loop ends (left > right), left has landed exactly at the position where target would need to be inserted — think about why.
No special-casing is needed beyond returning left instead of -1.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Scan linearly, returning the first index where nums[i] >= target.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Binary search, returning left when the target isn't found — left has converged exactly to the correct insertion index.

sections.title: Sqrt(x)
sections.problemStatement: Given a non-negative integer x, return the integer square root of x (the decimal part is truncated).
hints: This isn't binary search over an array — it's binary search over the ANSWER itself, a candidate value from 0 to x.

For a candidate mid, checking mid*mid <= x tells you whether mid could still be the answer or a smaller one is needed — the same 'discard half' logic applies to a range of possible answers, not just an array.
Track the largest mid found so far where mid*mid <= x — that's the floor square root once the search narrows to nothing left to check.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every integer from 0 upward until one squared exceeds x.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Binary search on the answer range [0, x], keeping the largest candidate whose square doesn't exceed x.

sections.title: Valid Perfect Square
sections.problemStatement: Given a positive integer num, return true if it is a perfect square.
hints: This is Sqrt(x)'s binary-search-on-the-answer idea again — search for a candidate whose square equals num exactly.
Binary search the range [1, num], checking mid*mid against num.
If the search ends without ever finding an exact match, num isn't a perfect square.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every integer from 1 upward, checking if its square equals num.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Binary search the range [1, num] for a candidate whose square exactly equals num.
